flashcards.front: How do you insert at the beginning of a linked list?
flashcards.back: Create new node, set newNode->next = head, update head = newNode. Time: O(1).

flashcards.front: How do you insert at the end of a linked list?
flashcards.back: Create new node, traverse to last node, set lastNode->next = newNode. Time: O(n).

flashcards.front: How do you delete the first node?
flashcards.back: Save head in temp, update head = head->next, free(temp). Time: O(1).

flashcards.front: How do you delete the last node?
flashcards.back: Traverse to second-to-last node, free its next, set its next to NULL. Time: O(n).

flashcards.front: How do you search for a value in a linked list?
flashcards.back: Start from head, compare each node's data with the target, move to next until found or NULL reached. Time: O(n).

flashcards.front: How do you display all nodes in a linked list?
flashcards.back: Start from head, print each node's data, move to next until NULL. Time: O(n).

flashcards.front: How do you reverse a linked list iteratively?
flashcards.back: Use three pointers (prev=NULL, curr=head, next). For each node: save next, reverse link (curr->next=prev), advance prev and curr.

flashcards.front: What edge cases must be handled in linked list operations?
flashcards.back: Empty list (head==NULL), single node (head->next==NULL), invalid position for insertion/deletion.

flashcards.front: Why must we free deleted nodes?
flashcards.back: To prevent memory leaks. Dynamically allocated memory (malloc) must be explicitly freed when no longer needed.

flashcards.front: How do you insert at a specific position?
flashcards.back: Traverse to node at (position-1), set newNode->next = temp->next, then temp->next = newNode. Time: O(n).
